1: Moses in the far wilderness — the future deliverer tending another man's flock in obscurity. A shadow: Jesus spent thirty years in Nazareth in carpenter's work before his public commissioning. The deliverer's public ministry is preceded by obscure, patient labor. Deuteronomy 18:15's promise of a prophet like Moses — the promise grounding the entire OT prophetic tradition — is fulfilled by the one who began, like Moses, in an unrecognized shepherding work before the divine commission came.
2: The burning bush — fire that burns without consuming, the theophany at the mountain. A shadow and type: the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:1-8) is the NT burning bush — the divine glory blazing through Jesus' human form, the fire of deity burning in the bush of flesh without consuming it. In both events, the disciples/Moses are terrified and fall on their faces; in both, a divine voice commissions the witness. The bush that burns but is not consumed is the visual form of the Incarnation: the eternal fire of divine holiness dwelling in mortal flesh without destroying it.
3: Moses turns aside to investigate — the curiosity that opens into commissioning. A revelation of God: the divine approach is gentle, inviting investigation rather than commanding from a distance. Jesus invites approach in exactly the same way: 'Come to me' (Matthew 11:28), 'Follow me' (Mark 1:17), 'Come and see' (John 1:39). The burning bush does not blast Moses from afar; it draws him near and waits. The divine initiative that invites rather than coerces is the form of grace throughout Scripture.
4: 'Moses, Moses' — the double personal naming, the form of divine call. Throughout the NT, Jesus calls individuals by name: 'Mary' (John 20:16 — the resurrection encounter), 'Saul, Saul' (Acts 9:4 — the Damascus road), 'Simon, Simon' (Luke 22:31 — the warning). The double calling in the OT (Abraham, Abraham in Genesis 22:11; Samuel, Samuel in 1 Samuel 3:10) signals sovereign attention and urgent commission. 'Here I am' (hineni) — the response of total availability: the form of election is always this exchange of divine address and human response.
5: 'Remove your sandals — the place where you stand is holy ground.' In John 1:27, John the Baptist declares he is not worthy to remove the sandal of the one coming after him. The command at the burning bush reverses: Moses removes his sandals before the presence of YHWH; John refuses to remove the sandal of Jesus because Jesus' presence far exceeds the category that requires sandal-removal. The one before whom Moses removes his sandals is the one before whom John cannot presume to act as a slave.
6: A direct Christological argument: Jesus cites this verse in Matthew 22:32 / Mark 12:26 to prove the resurrection of the dead. His logic: YHWH says 'I am the God of Abraham' (present tense) — therefore Abraham is alive before God at the moment Moses stands at the bush. YHWH is not God of the dead but of the living. The burning bush is therefore not merely the site of Moses' commission but the scriptural proof, in Jesus' own teaching, that death does not end the relationship between YHWH and his covenant people.
7: 'I have surely seen... I have heard... I know their sufferings' — the threefold divine attention. A revelation of God: the compassionate, responsive God who sees and knows the suffering of his people is the God who becomes incarnate precisely to share and bear that suffering. Isaiah 53:3-4: the Servant is 'a man of suffering, and familiar with pain... he took up our pain and bore our suffering.' The God of Ex 3:7 does not merely see from above; in Christ he descends into the suffering he sees. The Incarnation is the fullest form of YHWH's 'I know their sufferings.'
8: A near-direct citation: 'I have come down to deliver them and to bring them up out of that land.' Jesus says in John 6:38: 'For I have come down from heaven, not to do my own will but the will of him who sent me.' The vocabulary is the same: divine descent, purposive delivery, bringing up and out. John's entire prologue (Exodus 1:1-18) is structured around the pattern announced in Ex Exodus 3:8: the descent of the divine to deliver and bring up. The Exodus is not merely a historical parallel — it is the conceptual template for the Incarnation.
9: The cry of the Israelites has reached YHWH and he has seen their oppression — the legal appeal received and acknowledged. A revelation of God: the God who attends to the cry of the powerless is the God whose Son responds to every cry of need in the gospels. Blind Bartimaeus calls out and is not silenced (Mark 10:46-52); the ten lepers cry from a distance (Luke 17:11-13); Jairus falls at Jesus' feet (Mark 5:22-23). The God who heard the cry of enslaved Israel hears every human cry through the one in whom he became fully human.
10: 'Come, I will send you to Pharaoh' — the commissioning of Moses as shaliach (sent one). A type: Jesus is consistently identified as the one sent by the Father (John 3:17, Exodus 5:24, Exodus 6:38-40, Exodus 17:3). The chain of sending runs from Ex 3:10 through the apostolate: 'As the Father has sent me, I am sending you' (John 20:21). Deuteronomy 18:15 promises that YHWH will raise up a prophet like Moses — one who speaks YHWH's words as his commissioned representative, the ultimate sent one whose words are the Father's own (John 14:10).
11: 'Who am I?' — Moses' objection based on inadequacy. A revelation of God by contrast: Jesus never asks 'who am I?' His identity is given, not sought (Matthew 3:17: 'This is my Son, whom I love'). Moses points forward to the one whose commission requires no qualification — not 'who am I to go?' but 'I and the Father are one' (John 10:30). Hebrews 3:1-6 makes the comparison explicit: Moses was faithful in God's house as a servant; Jesus is faithful as the Son over the house. The servant's inadequacy points to the Son's sufficiency.
12: The sign that comes after, not before — faith required before evidence arrives. A shadow: the structure of all Christian discipleship mirrors this pattern. Jesus calls the disciples to follow before they fully understand (Mark 1:17-20); the resurrection is the retrospective sign that confirms the mission, not the advance proof that enables it. Hebrews 11:27 (Moses) and the whole gallery of faith in Heb 11 are structured around this pattern: acting on the unseen promise, receiving the confirmation after. The burning bush sign is the template of faith.
13: Moses asks what name to give when Israel asks who sent him — the demand for divine identification. The question anticipates the NT's central dispute about Jesus' identity. 'Who are you?' is asked of Jesus repeatedly (John 8:25, Mark 11:28). His answer, grounded in the divine name revealed here, is the claim that divides: 'I am' (John 8:58). The question of Ex Exodus 3:13 — by whose authority and in whose name? — is the question the religious authorities ask of Jesus at every turn (Mark 11:28: 'By what authority are you doing these things?').
14: A direct and foundational Christological text: 'I AM WHO I AM' (Ehyeh asher ehyeh) — the divine name revealed to Moses at the burning bush. Jesus applies the absolute 'I am' (ego eimi) to himself in John 8:58: 'Before Abraham was, I am' — the crowd recognizes the claim and takes up stones (John 8:59). The seven 'I am' statements in John (bread of life, light of the world, gate, good shepherd, resurrection and life, way/truth/life, true vine) are elaborations of what the name means concretely. The divine name of Ex 3:14 is the name Jesus claims as his own throughout John's Gospel.
15: YHWH as the covenant name, the name that is memorial throughout all generations — a direct connection to the NT's treatment of the name of Jesus. Philippians 2:9-11: God gave him 'the name that is above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow.' Acts 4:12: 'there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved.' The name YHWH that was Israel's exclusive covenant memorial (Ex Exodus 3:15) is, in the NT's understanding, borne by Jesus — the one in whom YHWH's character and covenant are fully disclosed and embodied.
16: Gather the elders of Israel — Moses convenes Israel's representative leadership before his mission begins. A type: Jesus gathers the twelve apostles as the representative reconstitution of Israel's twelve tribes (Matthew 19:28). The assembly of the elders in Ex 3:16 is the OT counterpart of the calling of the twelve: in both cases, the deliverer's first act is to constitute a representative body through whom the covenant community will be organized and the mission carried out.
17: The land of milk and honey — the inheritance promised to Abraham and now being actualized. A shadow: the promised land is a type of the ultimate inheritance of the people of God. Hebrews 11:13-16 makes this explicit — the patriarchs welcomed the promises from afar and acknowledged they were strangers on the earth, looking forward to a better country, the heavenly one. 1 Peter 1:4 describes the Christian inheritance as 'imperishable, undefiled, and unfading, reserved in heaven for you' — what Canaan was typologically, the new creation is permanently. Jesus comes to give his people not a land but himself as their dwelling (John 14:2-3).
18: 'Let us go a three-day journey to sacrifice to YHWH our God' — the three-day journey for sacrifice. A shadow: the three-day pattern in the OT is consistently connected to deliverance and resurrection (Genesis 22:4 — Abraham sees the mountain on the third day; Jonah 1:17 — three days in the fish; Hosea 6:2 — on the third day he will restore us). Jesus applies the Jonah sign explicitly to his own death and resurrection (Matthew 12:40). The three-day journey to the place of sacrifice in Ex 3:18 is a small shadow of the three days between the cross and the empty tomb.
19: YHWH foreknows that Pharaoh will resist — the hardened heart of the earthly ruler. A revelation of God: YHWH's purposes are not blocked by human resistance; they are accomplished through it. Romans 9:17 applies this directly: 'I raised you up for this very purpose, that I might display my power in you' (citing Ex Exodus 9:16). The hardening of Pharaoh's heart demonstrates that the powers of this age cannot finally resist the divine purpose — the same principle Paul applies to the cross: the rulers of this age crucified the Lord of glory, thus unknowingly accomplishing the purpose they sought to prevent (1 Corinthians 2:8).
20: The wonders YHWH will perform against Egypt — the signs and miracles that authenticate the divine commission and break the power of the oppressor. A theme: Jesus performs signs and wonders that authenticate his messianic commission (John 10:25, Exodus 20:30-31). The purpose of both the Exodus signs (demonstrating YHWH's sovereignty over Egypt's gods) and Jesus' signs (demonstrating the kingdom's arrival) is the same: to show that the power of the deliverer exceeds the power of every opposing force. The plagues dethrone Pharaoh; the miracles of Jesus dethrone the power of sin, disease, and death.
21: YHWH will cause the Egyptians to look favorably on Israel — divine reversal of the social order. A revelation of God: the God who reverses social hierarchies in favor of the covenant community. The Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55) is the NT statement of this principle: 'he has scattered those who are proud... he has brought down rulers from their thrones but has lifted up the humble... he has filled the hungry with good things but has sent the rich away empty.' What YHWH does for Israel in Egypt, he does definitively for all humanity in Christ.
22: The plundering of Egypt — Israel leaves with silver, gold, and clothing from their Egyptian neighbors. A shadow: Colossians 2:15 describes the cross as the plundering of the powers and authorities: 'Having disarmed the powers and authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross.' What Egypt's wealth was to Israel's exodus, the spoils of the cross are to the church's liberation: the very resources of the enemy become the endowment of the delivered people. The exodus that began with a death decree ends with the covenant community enriched at the oppressor's expense.
